basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Zalzala — “The Earthquake” — takes its name from the verb zalzala (زَلْزَلَ), an intensive reduplication that mimes, in its very shape, what it names: the shaking. Eight short verses. Five for the earth that quakes, empties its burdens, and — unheard-of thing — becomes a narrator. Three for the measure: any mote's weight of good, seen; any mote's weight of evil, seen. Tustarī cites Ibn Masʿūd: “The believer sees his sins as a man at the foot of a mountain that he fears will fall on him; the denier sees sin as a fly that lights on his nose — and with one wave of the hand, he chases it away.” And Sahl al-Tustarī warns: hold no sin small, for “four things that follow a sin are graver than the sin itself: to persist in it, to rejoice in it, to make light of it, to take pride in it.” The sūra, in its miniature, turns over two grounds: the one under our feet, and the one of our evaluations.

versets.trad: When the earth is shaken with her shaking,
versets.commentaire: zulzilat (زُلْزِلَتْ): passive of an intensive reduplicative verb — the repeated root mimes the shaking. zilzālahā (زِلْزَالَهَا), “her own shaking”, adds the possessive: each thing will tremble with the very shaking destined for it. The earth, which seemed the most stable thing, will be touched at its center. Spiritual reading: before the outer earth trembles, the inner earth — the ground of acquired certainties — is called to let itself be shaken. The path begins with that consented quaking.

mots.def: temporal particle introducing the circumstance: when, at the moment that.
zalzala: to shake violently; in the passive perfect feminine, subject: the earth.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

mots.def: arḍ: the earth, the ground, the planet.
zilzāl: verbal noun of zalzala, “the shaking”, “the quaking”; with feminine suffix pronoun: her own shaking.

versets.trad: and the earth casts forth her burdens,
versets.commentaire: athqāl (أَثْقَال): the burdens — what the earth carries that weighs, dead and buried treasures alike. All that men have hidden in her is given back, but in a way that undoes its use: gold, says tradition, will issue forth in columns; the murderer will see the gold and recognise: “For this I killed” — and no one will take it. What was the motive ceases to be an object of desire. Qushayrī sees in this detail a larger image: what will be brought into the open on the last Day is already, today, of no value for the heart that sees clearly.

mots.def: conjunction: and.
akhraja: to bring out, to expel, to yield up; feminine perfect.
arḍ: the earth.
athqāl: plural of thiql, burden, load; what the earth carries that weighs, including her dead and her treasures.

versets.trad: and man cries out: “What ails her?”
versets.commentaire: mā lahā (مَا لَهَا) — “what ails her?”. The usual expression of astonishment. Qushayrī specifies: this is the denier's question, the question of the one who had not believed in this hour. The believer does not wonder — he recognises. All his life he has lived awaiting this moment when the earth would tremble; when it comes, he simply says: here it is. Astonishment, here, is the sign of a heart that nothing had prepared.

mots.def: conjunction: and.
qāla: to say; perfect with future sense in the circumstance.
insān: man, the human being.
interrogative: what?
preposition li- + feminine suffix pronoun: to her, for her.

versets.trad: That Day she shall convey her chronicles,
versets.commentaire: The verb tuḥaddithu (تُحَدِّثُ) is of the same root as ḥadīth, the words reported from the Prophet. The earth becomes a narrator — she “tells her ḥadīths”. Tradition glosses: she will tell of each creature what was done upon her back. Nothing set down upon the soil has escaped the soil. For the Sufi the verse already speaks today: the steps one takes upon the earth are a writing being inscribed. To walk in presence (ḥuḍūr, حُضُور) is to know oneself read by the very ground.

mots.def: yawm + idh: that day, on that very day.
ḥaddatha: intensive of ḥadatha (to happen, to occur); to tell, to convey a narrative. Same root as ḥadīth.
akhbār: plural of khabar, news, account, chronicle; with feminine suffix pronoun: her news.

versets.trad: for your Lord shall have inspired her.
versets.commentaire: awḥā (أَوْحَىٰ): the very verb that names prophetic revelation (waḥy, وَحْي) — God inspires the earth as He inspired the prophets, as He inspires the bee (16:68). The whole creation is in subtle commerce with its Lord; there is no mute in God's house. Qushayrī: what the earth will say, she will not say on her own initiative — she only returns what has been entrusted to her. Her faithfulness as witness is to add nothing.

mots.def: preposition bi-: by; introduces cause or means.
subordinating conjunction: that.
rabb + suffix pronoun: your Lord.
awḥā: to inspire, to communicate subtly, to reveal; same root as waḥy, prophetic revelation.
preposition li- + feminine suffix pronoun.

versets.ar: يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصْدُرُ النَّاسُ أَشْتَاتًا لِّيُرَوْا أَعْمَالَهُمْ
versets.tr: yawmaʾidhin yaṣduru n-nāsu ashtātan li-yuraw aʿmālahum
versets.trad: That Day mankind shall come forth in scattered groups, that their deeds may be shown to them.
versets.commentaire: ashtātan (أَشْتَاتًا): scattered, separated. Tustarī gives a remarkable spiritual reading of this verse: “Each person will follow what he had relied on. Whoever relied on God's grace (faḍl, فَضْل) will follow His grace. Whoever relied on his own works will follow his works. Whoever relied on intercession (shafāʿa, شَفَاعَة) will follow the intercession.” The verse is no longer only a resurrection scene: it becomes a mirror held to the present life. On what are you leaning, right now? That is what will carry you that Day. The wise man therefore chooses to lean, today, on what will not break.

mots.def: see v. 4.
ṣadara: to come forth, to issue out, to go forward; imperfect singular (verb before a plural subject).
an-nās: mankind.
ashtāt: plural of shatīt, scattered, separated; here, each going his own way.
li- + yuraw: preposition of purpose + passive subjunctive plural; “that it may be shown to them”.
mots.gram: preposition + passive subjunctive verb, 3rd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: aʿmāl: plural of ʿamal, deed, work; with plural suffix pronoun.

versets.ar: فَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْرًا يَرَهُ
versets.tr: fa-man yaʿmal mithqāla dharratin khayran yarah
versets.trad: So whoever has done a mote's weight of good shall see it,
versets.commentaire: dharra (ذَرَّة) names in old Arabic the tiny ant, or the dust seen in a sunbeam — the smallest unit conceivable. When this verse came down, the Prophet preached, Tustarī reports: “This world has its goods at hand, eaten by the upright and the dissolute alike. But the Hereafter is the appointed hour when the All-powerful King will give His decree. All of the good, to its last detail, is in Paradise; all of the evil, to its last detail, is in the Fire. Be warned. Whoever has done a mote's weight of good will see it.” And Abū al-Dardāʾ adds: “The perfection of taqwā (تَقْوى) is that the servant fears God down to a mote's weight — to the point that he leaves aside certain things even held to be lawful, lest they be a veil before what is forbidden.”

mots.def: linking particle.
relative/conditional pronoun: whoever.
ʿamila: to do, to act; apocopated imperfect after the conditional.
mots.gram: apocopated imperfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: mithqāl: weight, measure; in the construct accusative: “the weight of”.
dharra: in old Arabic, the smallest thing conceivable — the tiny ant or dust in a sunbeam; today rendered as “atom”.
khayr: the good; indefinite accusative.
raʾā: to see; apocopated imperfect + suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: apocopated imperfect + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.ar: وَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُ
versets.tr: wa-man yaʿmal mithqāla dharratin sharran yarah
versets.trad: and whoever has done a mote's weight of evil shall see it.
versets.commentaire: The symmetry is exact: everything, in truth, is seen. Sahl al-Tustarī insists: hold no sin small. “Four things that follow a sin are graver than the sin itself: to persist in it, to rejoice in it, to make light of it (istiṣghār, اسْتِصْغَار), to take pride in it.” The sin known as a sin keeps a door of return open; the one that is minimised closes upon itself. Conversely, the good “of a mote's weight” — a kind word, half a date given, a smile — is not lost. God's measure is finer than ours; it weighs where we have not even thought to weigh.

mots.def: conjunction: and.
relative/conditional pronoun.
ʿamila: to do.
mots.gram: apocopated imperfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: mithqāl: see v. 7.
dharra: see v. 7.
sharr: evil; indefinite accusative.
raʾā: to see.
mots.gram: apocopated imperfect + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.
